source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 12.5 symbols and execution (pp. 216-217)

questions.q: What do the crontab time symbols *, -, , and / mean?
options: * = all values, - = a continuous range, , = a discontinuous list, / = an interval (step)
questions.why: * every value, - a continuous range (11-16), , a discrete list (11,16), / a step interval (*/10).
The comma list 11,16 in the hour field means it runs at both 11:00 and 16:00 each day.

options: Once an hour, on the hour, from 11:00 to 16:00 every day
On days 11 to 16 of the month
questions.why: The range 11-16 in the hour field (minute 00) fires on the hour every hour from 11:00 through 16:00.

options: Every 10 minutes, from 08:00 to 17:00, Monday to Friday
At 10 past every hour, all week
questions.why: */10 = every 10 minutes, 8-17 = the working hours, 1-5 = Monday to Friday (day-of-week).

options: Runs the command daily just after midnight
Runs the command once at reboot
questions.why: @daily runs just after midnight each day; @weekly is every Sunday after midnight, @monthly the 1st, @annually 1 January.

options: Runs a command when the system reboots (starts up)
Runs a command just before shutdown
questions.why: @reboot schedules the command to run at system startup.

questions.q: How does the crond daemon process scheduled tasks?
options: It reads all users' task files every minute, runs due tasks, and writes events to /var/log/cron
It reads tasks only at boot and logs to /etc/cron
It runs tasks every second and logs to the terminal
It waits for the user to trigger each task manually
questions.why: crond reads the schedule files every minute, runs whatever is due, and logs events/messages to /var/log/cron.
